reasoning: The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) sets the target range for the federal funds rate, which virtually determines the DFF. The Fed has been in an easing cycle, cutting rates steadily in 2025 until holding and stabilizing the DFF at 3.64% since late December 2025. At the January 28, 2026 meeting, the rate was held steady, but there were two dissents strongly advocating for an additional cut, signaling a dovish bias. The next FOMC meeting will take place on March 17-18, 2026. Because this meeting occurs after the first resolution date, no policy change can occur before March 8, meaning the rate will almost certainly remain completely flat at exactly 3.64% (absent an unprecedented random daily 1 bp spike). By the second resolution date (March 31), the FOMC will have met. Current market consensus confidently forecasts either a rate hold or another rate cut; a rate hike is extraordinarily unlikely given the ongoing economic trajectory. With a target range increase virtually impossible and month-end/quarter-end reporting fluctuations more likely to cause minor dips rather than spikes, any strict increase (>3.64%) on either date is highly improbable. The probability is set at the minimum allowable thresholds, slightly increasing for the latter date.
